data.title: Gold's Long-Term Expected Return
data.description:
Gold's real return - greater than you thought

While gold’s contribution to managing portfolio risk is well established, supported by a large body of work devoted to its hedging characteristics,1 its contribution to portfolio return is not. Frameworks for estimating gold’s long-term return exist but fall short of a robust approach that aligns with the capital market assumptions for other asset classes. This report sets out such a framework, accounting for gold’s unique dual nature as a real good and a financial asset.

Publications tackling gold’s expected return have generally concluded that gold’s primary function is as a store of value, implying a long-run co-movement of gold with the general price level (CPI). Alternative approaches using risk premia estimations or bond-like structures with embedded options produce similar results.

And while existing research is rich in insight, two features frequently pop up that, in our view, mischaracterise gold and have led to biased conclusions:

  • Using data from periods during the Gold Standard to analyse gold’s performance paints a misleading relationship between gold and general prices2
  • Viewing long-term price dynamics exclusively through the lens of demand from financial markets and ignoring other sources of demand, is a likely contributor to a systematic underweighting of gold in private portfolio allocations.

In most cases, these theories land on an expected long-run real return ranging between 0% and 1%.3

We instead show that gold’s long-run return has been well above inflation for over 50 years (Figure 1), more closely mirroring global gross domestic product (GDP), a proxy for the economic expansion driver used in our other gold pricing models.

Our simple yet robust approach – which we refer to as Gold Long-Term Expected Return or GLTER – uses the distribution of above-ground gold stocks analysed via different demand categories as a foundation and starting point.

The drivers of gold buyers across various demand segments – jewellery and technology fabrication, central banks, financial investment, retail bars and coins4 – are crucially broader and more important than existing theories suggest.5 In addition, although financial market investors tend to dictate price formation in the short term, they are less dominant in the long term.6

We show that the gold price over long horizons is mainly driven by an economic component, proxied by global nominal GDP, coupled with a financial component, proxied by the capitalisation of global stock and bond markets, that balances the overall relationship. Third-party inputs are then used to estimate long-term expected returns for gold.7

Figure 1: Gold’s return over the past 50 years has been in line with global GDP and well above inflation

Annual growth in US CPI, global nominal GDP, and gold price (1971–2023)

Footnotes

1. For a discussion of this research see O’Connor (2015)

2. Prior to the closure of the gold window, gold was primarily a monetary asset that could only be bought and sold at its official price in most jurisdictions.

3. The most commonly applied deflator to achieve real returns is the US consumer price index.

4. Defined as purchases of bars and coins less than 1kg in a retail setting. See Supply and Demand notes and definitions.

5. Further details of the data can be found here.

6. In reality the delineation between short term and long term isn’t easily done, suffice to say that supply chain buffers delay transmission to price from some demand sectors such as jewellery, retail bar and coin and technology.

7. This report does not purport to forecast the gold price or future performance of gold. This report sets out a proposed methodology and highlights the expected long-term returns for gold utilising third-party input assumptions. The World Gold Council does not make any recommendation or suggestion as to appropriateness of particular inputs into the model. Users can apply different inputs, which will generate different long-term expected returns for gold.

Disclaimer

Important information and disclaimers

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: Buy And Hold This Stock Forever
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) reported earnings last week to great fanfare, as the stock added a 5+% rally on top of a share price that has already more than doubled over the last 12 months. Even after this run-up, TSM remains one of my favorite picks for the simple reason that it has all the hallmarks of a business to own forever: it has a wide moat, is positioned in an essential and growing sector, and has demonstrated unparalleled operational excellence. Buy TSM and don't let go.

With AI demand continuing to surge and stock valuations soaring, expectations were fairly high for TSMC's Q3 results: as the world's pre-eminent chip manufacturer, servicing almost all the leading semiconductor design companies, TSMC is a bellwether for the health of the tech sector and the economy at large. Even so, the company was able to surpass those lofty expectations and further fuel investor optimism over its ability to maintain its dominant market position. Let's recap the most recent earnings results.

TSMC reported quarterly revenue of $23.5 billion, up 39% YoY and 12% QoQ, EPS of $1.94, up 54% YoY and 31% QoQ, and gross margin of 57.8%, up 350 bps YoY and 450 bps QoQ. As far as core metrics go, not many companies are achieving this level of growth on a revenue base this high with a margin this lucrative. Contract manufacturing in most industries is a low-margin race to the bottom. However, TSMC is so far ahead of the competition, which is really just Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Intel Corporation (INTC), that it has been able to consistently achieve an outsized net profit margin, including a record-high 42.8% for Q3 2024.

On the balance sheet side, the company is in great shape as cash & equivalents have ballooned to nearly $60 billion, up 44% YoY, compared to just $28 billion in long-term debt, which is actually slightly down from Q3 2023. And while TSMC's business is highly capital intensive, the cash flows look pristine as well with $5.7 billion in free cash flow, adding to the stockpile and easily covering the $2.8 billion quarterly dividend payment.

Guidance was equally upbeat with revenue expected between $26.1 billion and $26.9 billion, up 35% YoY and 13% QoQ at the midpoint, and margins expected to be flat or see a slight increase.

When it comes to TSMC, you can usually get a finger on the pulse of management's expectations by following capital expenditures, which the company ramps up and down based on the demand profile for the coming quarters. In that vein, TSMC boosted CapEx guidance for FY2024 to be slightly above its original $30 billion estimate and projected, without specifying a number, that FY2025 CapEx will likely be higher still.

For those following the semiconductor space, this steady march higher in spending should perhaps come as no surprise. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) is in the midst of launching its next-generation Blackwell AI chips, the demand for which CEO Jensen Huang characterized as “insane.” Apple Inc. (AAPL) is placing massive orders for capacity on TSMC's most cutting-edge node (N3) for its iPhones and Mac laptops. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is using TSMC for its various CPU and AI accelerator offerings. QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) has booked capacity for its Snapdragon mobile chips, and even Intel contracted out its Arrow Lake processors to TSMC after ditching volume production on its 20A process node.

This who's who of semiconductor design behemoths demonstrates just how far ahead TSMC is compared to the competition: the world's largest companies fight over its capacity for their most advanced SoCs and pay top dollar for it. And while TSMC has demonstrated operational excellence, Intel and Samsung have faltered recently. After being the undisputed king of the foundry for decades, Intel flubbed its 10nm process (re-branded Intel 7), eliminating what was viewed as an insurmountable moat. For an article that chronicles this downfall contemporaneously, you can read my piece about Intel's 10nm debacle from 2019 here.

Intel has shown progress recently with a more aggressive approach to node development timelines, which, I think, has resulted in what could very well be a competitive node in 2025, dubbed 20A. However, TSMC has now established a technological lead, an operational lead, and a customer lead that won't be easily disrupted.

As for Samsung, though it remains a leader in memory chip manufacturing, logic chips have been a tougher nut to crack. After previously boasting Qualcomm as one of its major customers, Samsung was unable to sufficiently scale its 3nm process to Qualcomm's satisfaction, prompting the latter to move all major orders to TSMC. It was further reported last week that Samsung had delayed taking shipment of EUV lithography machines, which are used in the manufacturing of advanced chips, from Dutch company ASML Holding N.V. (ASML). These were originally intended for Samsung's upcoming fab in Texas, which has also seen some on-site staff from the company sent back to South Korea.

At this point, Intel and Samsung are fighting over whatever scraps are left on the table as TSMC continues to massively expand manufacturing capacity to capture as much of the market as possible. And once that market share is captured and customers are locked in, it's difficult to pry them away. Due to this significant lead, the ever-expanding demand for semiconductor manufacturing, and the operational excellence the company continues to demonstrate, I'm having a hard time thinking of a company that is more set for long-term success than TSMC.

Whether it's generative AI, smartphones, servers, or whatever the next five or ten secular trends appear to entail, TSMC is positioned to profit from whatever comes down the pipe. Yes, there is the risk that competitors will catch up and margins will fall off slightly. Or, that an economic recession will cripple end-user demand and cause major capacity utilization issues. AI could end up being massively scaled back due to a lack of applications. However, there will always be someone willing to pay a hefty sum for a contract manufacturer that can deliver on the bleeding edge, on time, and within specifications.

With how much cash TSMC is pulling in, I expect to see a steady increase in the dividend over the foreseeable future:

It has already begun creeping up, but with the payout ratio falling and cash piling up on the balance sheet, I expect the company to begin returning capital to shareholders at a higher rate. In fact, one of my main criticisms would be the current reluctance to spend cash for management, so I'll be keeping a watchful eye on that in the coming quarters and years.

While the valuation might be considered rich at a forward P/E of around 28, the high revenue base paired with robust growth and best-in-class margins makes the stock attractive. Beyond just next year's earnings, TSMC's market position is perhaps one of the best of any company in the market today and I intend to hold my shares for a very long time, if not forever.

Just to cover all bases, also announced last week was a U.S. Commerce Department probe of TSMC over possibly selling or manufacturing chips for Chinese giant Huawei, either directly or through third parties. While this is something investors should note, it's highly unlikely this will result in a material impact beyond a possible fine if TSMC hardware somehow found its way to Huawei.

Investor Takeaway

TSMC is the undisputed leader in a sector that will be critical for corporate and national security interests for decades to come. The company is generating record revenue, earnings, margins, and cash flow on the back of secular trends like AI and will continue to benefit whatever the next trend may be. Operational excellence and unparalleled execution have made and will continue to make TSMC the go-to foundry of choice for the world's top tech companies.

While the stock may waffle or drop in the coming years, whether due to a dip in AI demand or the specter of an economic recession or the arrival of an actual economic recession, I will use that as an opportunity to add to my position. I'm rating TSM a Strong Buy, and it's a stock I intend to own forever.

Thanks for reading!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pony AI Pursues U.S. IPO Amid Growth And Heavy Losses
data.description:
Pony AI Is Growing, But Operating Losses Remain High

Pony AI Inc (PONY) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its American Depositary Shares representing underlying Class A ordinary shares, according to an SEC F-1 registration statement.

Pony is commercializing autonomous passenger and truck vehicle transportation technologies and services.

The company is producing topline revenue growth but at the cost of high operating losses and cash burn, and it faces broad risks across major areas of its operations, competitive landscape and structure.

When we learn more IPO details from management, I’ll provide a final opinion.

What Does Pony AI Do?

Hengli, China-based Pony AI was founded to develop driverless passenger cars and trucks to operate in China.

Management is headed by co-founder, Chairman and CEO Dr. Jun Peng, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2016 and was previously Chief Architect at Baidu (BIDU) and was also a software engineer at Google (GOOG) (GOOGL).

The company’s primary offerings include the following:

  • Robotaxis for passenger customers

  • Autonomous trucks for freight operations

  • Licensing and applications

As of June 30, 2024, Pony has booked fair market value investment of $1.43 billion from investors, including Toyota Motor Corporation, HongShan, 2774719 Ontario Limited, Dr. Tiancheng Lou, IDG entities and 5Y Capital entities.

The firm currently operates approximately 250 robotaxis in certain provinces in China and over 190 autonomous trucks for freight operations.

Pony claims over 220,000 consumer users of its PonyPilot app and says its vehicles have driven more than 39 million kilometers of autonomous driving mileage in a variety of geographies.

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower as revenues have grown, as the figures below indicate:

Selling, G&A

Expenses vs. Revenue

Period

Percentage

Six Mos. Ended June 30, 2024

63.0%

2023

52.0%

2022

71.9%

(Source - SEC)

The Selling, G&A efficiency multiple, defined as how many dollars of additional new revenue are generated by each dollar of Selling, G&A expense, rose markedly in the most recent reporting period, as shown in the table below:

Selling, G&A

Efficiency Rate

Period

Multiple

Six Mos. Ended June 30, 2024

0.8

2023

0.1

(Source - SEC)

Market Overview For Pony AI

According to a 2024 market research report by Renub Research, the market in China for autonomous vehicles was an estimated $8 billion in 2023 and is expected to reach $31.6 billion by 2030.

This represents a forecast CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of an impressive 21.7% from 2024 to 2030.

The main drivers for this expected growth are a continued growth in urban population, lower potential costs for driverless vehicles and a friendly regulatory environment for such technologies in China.

Various competitive or other industry participants include the following:

  • AutoX

  • Baidu Apollo

  • Didi Chuxing

  • TuSimple

  • WeRide

  • SAIC Lab

  • XPeng

  • Inceptio

Also, China has produced nationwide guidelines for autonomous vehicle usage in public transportation areas and by 2025, 'half of all new cars sold in the nation are expected to have some form of self-driving technology.'

My SWOT thumbnail for Pony is described here:

  • Strengths

    • Significant mileage history and data collection, diverse geographic coverage, positive regulatory stance

  • Weaknesses

    • Dependence on third-party OEMs and service providers, previous VIE legal structure

  • Opportunities

    • Increasing acceptance of various levels of autonomous driving technologies among consumers and businesses

  • Threats

    • Competition from much larger entities with platform advantages, increasing losses and uncertain ability to obtain capital from international sources

Pony’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Growing topline revenue

  • Uneven gross profit and reduced gross margin

  • High operating losses and cash used in operations

Below are major financial results from the firm’s current registration statement:

(Source - SEC)

As of June 30, 2024, Pony had $473 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investments and $44.5 million in total liabilities.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2024, was negative ($116 million).

IPO Details For Pony AI

Pony intends to raise $100 million in gross proceeds from an IPO of its American Depositary Shares representing underlying Class A ordinary shares, although the final figure may be as high as $300 million.

There haven’t been any existing shareholders or potentially new investors who have indicated an interest in purchasing shares at the IPO price.

The firm is an ‘emerging growth company’ and a ‘foreign private issuer’, which means management can choose to disclose substantially less information in the IPO and as a public company.

Many such company stocks have provided poor returns for investors after the IPO.

Also, it is common for Asia-based companies to limit communication to a very low level, reducing visibility into their operations and outlook.

Management disclosed it intends to apply the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

for execution of our go-to market strategies in order to carry out the large-scale commercialization of our autonomous driving technology in our key addressable markets, including both robotaxi and robotruck services, including for business development, production, sales and marketing, customer services, and partnerships with industry participants;

for continued investments in research and development of our autonomous driving technology; and

for general corporate purposes, and potential strategic investments and acquisitions to strengthen our technological capabilities and overall ecosystem, although we have not identified any specific investments or acquisition opportunities at this time.

(Source - SEC)

The company's online roadshow isn’t available, which is common for Asia-based companies.

Regarding outstanding legal proceedings, management said that any claims would not have a material adverse effect on the company's business or financial condition.

The listed bookrunners of the IPO are Goldman Sachs [Asia], BofA Securities, Deutsche Bank, Huatai Securities and Tiger Brokers.

Pony AI Is Growing But Producing Heavy Operating Losses

PONY is seeking U.S. public capital market investment to fund its continued operations expansion within China and its R&D efforts.

The firm’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue, variable gross profit and reduced gross margin and heavy operating losses and cash used in operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2024, was negative ($116 million).

Selling, G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue have trended lower as revenue has increased; its Selling, G&A efficiency multiple rose substantially to 0.8x in the most recent half-year reporting period.

The firm currently plans to pay no dividends due to its extensive losses, which are likely to continue well into the future.

Pony’s recent capital spending results show that it has continued to spend on capital expenditures despite heavy operating cash flow usage.

The market opportunity for providing autonomous transportation services in China is large and expected to grow at a high rate of growth in the coming years, so Pony enjoys very positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

However, the company faces significant competition from much larger, deep-pocketed players and requires substantial additional capital to fund its heavy operating losses in the meantime.

For example, competitors Didi Chuxing has a large user base, and Baidu Apollo has extensive government partnerships in numerous provinces.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public company also include the arbitrary and unpredictable nature of Chinese regulatory practices, and U.S. investors would only have an interest in a Cayman Islands holding company and not equity interests in the underlying operating companies in China.

Additionally, Pony faces the potential of being identified as a ‘Commission-Identified Issuer’ with respect to its financial statements if the U.S. PCAOB cannot inspect or investigate the firm’s auditor under the HFCAA.

Such an identification, if it occurred, could jeopardize the PONY stock listing on U.S. markets.

Also, the firm has previously been found to have had a material weakness in its internal financial reporting due to a shortage of personnel familiar with U.S. GAAP accounting policies. I suspect this is not an uncommon challenge for Asia-based companies.

Pony also faces significant legal liability from its autonomous operations, and its ability to properly manage such risks will be an important factor in its profitability.

When we learn more IPO details regarding pricing and valuation assumptions, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Buy Before Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Exxon Mobil Corp. (NYSE:XOM) on May 7th, 2024 with 'Exxon Mobil: Not Cheap, Still A Buying Opportunity', the stock was in the process of rallying quite strongly (posting gains of over 29.2% in less than four months). Of course, these bullish trend movements were quite forceful and possibly surprising to some investors and this would have been due to the fact that those rallies actually followed a long series of losses (totaling more than -20.65%) that had also developed over a period less than four months:

In prior articles, I have discussed the possibility that this type of two-way price movement can actually signify a period of indecision in the market because this type of seemingly directionless trading activity can pave the way for sideways ranges in the future. As we can see in the chart below, Exxon Mobil's recent price behavior is an excellent example of this type of market phenomenon:

Ultimately, this sideways trading range lasted for nearly five months before breaking to the topside. The bullish breakout that occurred in early October was propelled in large part by strong second-quarter earnings results, which surpassed consensus estimates by a fairly wide margin and showed the highest oil production levels that have been seen at any point since the original Exxon / Mobil merger was finalized in 1999. For the period, the company recorded per-share earnings of $2.14 and this result soundly beat consensus estimates calling for $2.01 per share. On the revenue side, Exxon saw figures rise to $93.06 billion, and this was another substantial beat relative to the $90.99 billion that marked consensus estimates. Net income figures indicated annualized growth rates of 17% (at $9.2 billion) for the period.

Overall, it appears to be quite clear that Exxon's acquisition of Pioneer Natural Resources has had a dramatic impact on these positive results, contributing roughly $500 million to the quarterly earnings figure. On the negative side, YTD profit figures are showing losses of -9% (at $17.5 billion) relative to the prior-year figures, so it is also clear that weakened prices for natural gas and reduced margins for refining have had an obvious impact on Exxon's results. All of that said, it must be understood that Exxon saw a 15% quarterly gain in production levels (at 4.4 million barrels daily) and these improvements were supported by record production levels that were achieved in the Permian and Guyana.

Given these strong results, investors must also draw important comparisons between XOM and alternative strategic plays within the energy markets. Currently, Exxon's forward price-earnings valuation remains somewhat elevated (at 14.89x), which is roughly in-line with the market current valuation of Chevron Corp. (CVX) at 14.12x. Looking at the company's industry peer group, there is really only one name that stands out to me as it trades at more expensive levels, and this would be Marathon Petroleum Corp. (MPC) at 17.42x. Alternatively, two oil companies that are trading at much more attractive levels right now can be found, with Shell plc (SHEL) at 8.42x and BP (BP) at 8.25x.

Similar trends can be seen when we view these companies in terms of their forward price-sales valuations. In this case, Exxon is trading with an elevated 1.51x valuation, while Chevron is trading a bit lower with a ratio of 1.39x. Again, the other energy companies in this group are trading at much cheaper valuations with Shell seen at 0.699x, BP at 0.42x and Marathon Petroleum seen at just 0.39x.

However, if we value these companies based on the price-book value metric, Exxon starts to look slightly more attractive (at 1.99x). Here, Marathon Petroleum is more expensively valued at 2.49x, but the other three companies in this grouping are still trading at lower valuations. Specifically, Chevron is currently trading at 1.72x, while British Petroleum is seen at 1.23x and Shell is seen at 1.11x. Overall, this is probably the individual metric that places XOM in its best light, but investors must remember that Exxon is trading at (or near) the top of this grouping for each of these valuation metrics, and it is clear that other energy companies are currently trading with lower valuations at the moment.

Looking at near-term activity in XOM share prices, we can see some very significant movement has occurred recently. Specifically, I am referring to the sideways trading range breakout that was seen during the October 2nd trading session (shown in the chart below). Ultimately, this trading activity signals an end to the period of indecision that has largely been in place since the end of April. This directional break shows momentum is finally moving in the positive direction, and I think some of this optimism is centered around the fact that Exxon now looks to be strongly positioned to continue outperforming consensus expectations.

In order to downgrade from my current bullish outlook, I would need to see share prices break below $114.80 because this would suggest that the current wave of bullish momentum has completed. I am watching this area in particular because this region marks the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of the ascending move from the September 11th, 2024 lows at $107.77 to the October 7th, 2024 highs at $126.34 - and this price region aligns closely with support zones created by the September 20th lows at $114.25.

Ultimately, I would need to see share prices break below the stock's major long-term support zone of $107.77 in order to reverse to a more bearish bias. I am focusing on this area as my reversal point because this region essentially defines the bottom of Exxon's sideways trading range and a downward break through this area would then suggest that the bullish price move that was seen during the October 2nd trading session should actually be characterized as a false breakout. Currently, I am anticipating favorable results in Exxon's upcoming earnings release due to the size of the earnings and revenue beats that were seen in the Q2 report and the clearly positive influences that the Pioneer acquisition is having on the company's bottom line. Income investors should also be encouraged by Exxon's recent share buybacks ($5.2 billion) and the $4.3 billion in dividend payouts that were just completed by the company. As a result of all of these factors, I will maintain my long position and raise my rating to a "strong buy" based on the expectation that I think the stock still has the potential to move to new highs before the end of this year.

data.title: Terns Pharmaceuticals: Another Potential GLP-1 Entrant
data.description:
Shares of biotech concern Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:TERN) are up somewhat higher since we first examined it in September of 2022, riding optimism concerning two early-stage clinical programs. The company leveraged encouraging Phase 1 data from its oral obesity candidate TERN-601 to extend its cash runway into 2028 on September 10, 2024. With first-ever American patient CML data due in December 2024 and Phase 2 obesity study not set to commence until 2025, the recent insider buying merited a revisit.

When we last investigated Terns in August 2022, it had three early stage (then dubbed) non-alcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH)/obesity assets and a newly emphasized chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) program. The company’s very thinly traded stock was on a run, rallying to the mid-4s after trading at a significant discount to cash. Investment was not recommended due to a lack of catalysts over the ensuing twelve months.

As a reminder, Terns Pharmaceuticals, Inc. is a Foster City, California based early clinical-stage biopharmaceutical concern focused on the development of small-molecule therapeutics for indications with high unmet needs. The company currently has two programs targeting obesity and CML. Terns was formed in 2016 and backed by Eli Lilly (LLY), which out-licensed its NASH portfolio to the then startup, along with a Series A investment of $30 million in 2018. The company went public in February 2021, raising net proceeds of $132.9 million at $17 per share. The stock currently trades for just over seven bucks a share, translating to a market cap of $625 million.

Pipeline

Since the last report, Terns’ four-program clinical-stage portfolio is down to two prioritized assets.

TERN-701. The company’s program with scheduled data is TERN-701, an oral allosteric inhibitor of tyrosine kinase oncoprotein BCR-ABL1 (a.k.a. the Philadelphia chromosome) that is being evaluated in a Phase 1 trial (CARDINAL) for the treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Originating in the bone marrow, CML is responsible for ~15%, or ~9,300 of the newly diagnosed cases of leukemia in American adults annually. Current therapies in the $5 billion+ treatment market include active-site tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs), including generic Gleevec (imatinib, FY23 sales of $600 million), Novartis’ (NVS) Tasigna (nilotinib, $1.8 billion), Bristol-Myers Squibb’s (BMY) Sprycel (dasatinib, $2 billion), and Pfizer’s (PFE) Bosulif (bosutinib, $620 million). However, due to an increasing number of patients failing on active-site TKIs, there is an unmet need in the CML indication.

The primary reason patients are becoming more refractory to TKIs is active-site mutations that overcome or evade treatment. Furthermore, active-site TKIs are hampered by off-target toxicity. The only approved off-site (allosteric) TKI, Novartis’ Scemblix (asciminib), has demonstrated double the efficacy over first- and second-generation active-site TKIs because it binds to the myristoyl-binding pocket, inhibiting the BCR-ABL1 gene and most mutations, which are responsible for the proliferation of leukemia cells. It also is better tolerated than its predecessors with a 5% discontinuation rate versus 10%-11% for active-site TKIs. Green-lit by the FDA in October 2021, Scemblix achieved 1H24 sales of $300 million in its currently sanctioned third-line setting with multi-blockbuster potential if Novartis obtains approval for CML as a front-line therapy. On that front, the FDA has accepted its application and granted it priority review but has yet to assign a PDUFA date. However, due to certain factors, including twice-daily dosing, a three-hour fasting requirement, some potentially harmful drug interactions, and off-target toxicity, adherence on Scemblix is likely to be challenging. Terns believes that its allosteric candidate TERN-701 can overcome these limitations by better pharmacokinetics and a longer half-life, allowing for once-daily dosing with minimal fasting requirements. It is currently the only allosteric clinical competition to Scemblix.

In a Phase 1 study on healthy subjects, the candidate demonstrated the ability to be administered without regard to food and a median half-life of 8 to 12 hours, suggestive of once-daily dosing. Also, the pharmacokinetic profile of TERN-701 was consistent with an ongoing Phase 1 trial conducted by Chinese partner, Hansoh Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:HNSPF), who in-licensed the asset for development in China. Based on these developments, Terns initiated CARDINAL with interim data anticipated in December 2024. TERN-701 received orphan drug designation from the FDA in March 2024.

Returning to Hansoh, it initiated a two-part Phase 1 study in 2Q22 with the initial dose escalation phase to be followed by cytogenetic response evaluation at six months in part two. No timeline for data has been forwarded. Hansoh in-licensed TERN-701 for $1 million by exercising an option in 2021 with Terns eligible to receive potential milestones totaling $67 million and mid-single digit royalties.

TERN-601. The asset with fresh data that has created some buzz around the company is TERN-601. It is an oral small molecule glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor (GLP-1R) agonist and one of the more recent entrants into the white-hot obesity space. Oral administration with insignificant side effects is seen as the next evolutionary phase in this indication, and the first one to the finish line is likely to have a treatment with a potential 11-figure annual revenue stream in a global market estimated to grow to $130 billion by 2030, according to Goldman Sachs.

The early returns for TERN-601 were encouraging, when it was revealed on September 9, 2024 that its highest dose (740mg, n=9) demonstrated maximum placebo-adjusted mean weight loss of 4.9% (p<0.0001) (-5.5% absolute weight loss from baseline) at 28 days with two-thirds of the patients achieving at least 5% weight loss. It was well-tolerated with six of the nine patients experiencing Grade 2 adverse events but no Grade 3 AEs. This news rallied Terns’ stock 44% over the subsequent two trading sessions to a 52-week closing high of $11.23 share on September 10, 2024.

Although highly encouraging, it should be noted that Viking Therapeutics’ (VKTX) oral peptide dual GLP-1/GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide) receptor agonist candidate VK2735 delivered somewhat similar results at its highest dosage (40mg, n=7) with placebo-adjusted mean weight loss of 3.3% (p=0.0006) (-5.3% absolute weight loss from baseline) at 28 days in its Phase 1 study released in March 2024. A Phase 2 trial is planned for 4Q24. The subcutaneous version of VK2735 is heading for Phase 3 evaluation in 2025. Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) oral small molecule GLP-1 agonist CT-996 produced 6.1% placebo-adjusted mean weight loss in its best-performing cohort (n=6) at 28 days based on data first announced in July 2024. It is also headed for a Phase 2 trial.

These clinical rivals don’t include the two dominant players in the injectable space, both of which are pouring significant capital into their own oral therapies. Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) oral amycretin, which targets both GLP-1 and hunger-affecting pancreatic hormone amylin, produced an eye-popping 13.1% placebo-adjusted weight loss in its highest dose (100mg) at 12 weeks in a Phase 1 trial, which was a significant improvement over its oral semaglutide formulation that achieved a 12.7% placebo-adjusted reduction at 68 weeks while being administered on an empty stomach in a Phase 3 study. Eli Lilly’s orforglipron scored 12.4% placebo-adjusted weight loss at 36 weeks in its Phase 2 trial.

It is too early to tease out winners and significantly larger studies will further delineate the efficacy, tolerability, lean body mass reduction, and other metrics regarding these candidates. TERN-601 is expected to enter Phase 2 evaluation in 2025.

MASH De-Prioritization

The other two assets covered in the previous report, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) candidates TERN-101 and TERN-501, have been shelved after data from their studies were not convincing enough to successfully solicit partners to advance them further into the clinic.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Terns leveraged the positive obesity data from TERN-601 into a capital call, raising net proceeds of $161.9 million at $10.50 per share on September 10, 2024. When added to its cash and securities position of $225.3 million on June 30, 2024, the company should have an operating runway into 2028.

The Street is constructive on Terns’ prospects, featuring ratings and one hold rating since the beginning of September with a median price target in the high teens..

Also bullish is board member Hongbo Lu. Representing the interests of NEXTBio Capital, he used the recent secondary as an opportunity to purchase 476,910 shares of TERN at $10.50.

Verdict:

The current excitement generated from TERN-601 notwithstanding, over the next six to twelve months, Terns’ share price is a function of the Phase 1 CML data from TERN-701. The returns from that are somewhat of a crap shot given it is the first-ever study on American CML patients with only limited data from the Chinese study to date. As for TERN-601, it is in a heavily trafficked space with likely two Phase 3 studies costing $300 million in its future. That said, it is not entering Phase 2 development until 2025. As such, the ~$250 million valuation net of cash seems reasonable for now for a very small 'watch item' position but only for very aggressive investors.

data.title: Labrador Iron: China Stimulus Effects Fading Fast
data.description:
Note: All amounts are in Canadian Dollars unless noted otherwise. Canadian stock options are inaccessible by US Investors.

Despite weaker year over year iron ore prices, Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Corporation (TSX:LIF:CA, LIFZF) delivered higher net income and cash flow per share to its investors in the first quarter of this year. Run as a lean, mean machine, the company passed on the excess cash to its shareholders in the form of a bump in the second quarter dividends.

We covered those results. While all was hunky-dory in that quarter, we did not think the fundamentals supported a spillover of good times to the balance of the year. We expected the demand for ore to go down further still, and we were looking at China's slowing new real estate construction as a guidepost. We went with a hold rating on this royalty play.

With iron ore, we expect demand to ultimately drive down prices and force supply closures. Those will only happen at a sub US $70/ton price. Correspondingly, we would get more constructive on Labrador Iron in the $22.50-$25.00 range. For those bullish, we would suggest that the June 2025 covered calls would be the one to initiate at this point.

Source: Labrador Iron: Reviewing Our 19% Yield Options Trade.

The stock has done reasonably well since then, with a 3.25% return.

With the kickstarting of the Chinese economy, are we ready to get on this company's bandwagon? We will review the most recent results and provide our updated outlook next.

Q2-2024

Labrador iron benefited from a triple whammy of higher production, higher prices and a weaker Canadian dollar in Q2-2024. Relative to Q2-2023, its net income per share was up 20%. Other measures of shareholder return including cash flow and adjusted cash flow per share were up far more substantially.

The key driver here for cash flow (beyond the higher net income, of course) was the far larger set of dividends received from the IOC and lower cash taxes paid.

Labrador iron ended Q2-2024 with an extremely clean balance sheet. Current assets exceeded current liabilities, and the only long-term liabilities were the deferred income taxes.

Outlook

If you came here hoping for some extra bit of information on Labrador Iron's mines, you will be severely disappointed. For a royalty company like this one, the underlying commodity drives 60% of the returns. The company valuation probably drives the bulk of the rest. So iron prices are the key, and this is one commodity where almost all the exports, and a lion's share of the global output, is used by one country. China. Chinese residential and commercial usage of iron ore is what will determine the longer-term fate of Labrador Iron.

In this regard, investors have been fairly joyous over the recent stimulus measures. We are not sure. We believe the amount of stimulus is way lower than what is needed to even begin to turn things around. China's money supply appears to be in the doldrums.

Commercial and residential prices are moving sharply lower year over year.

As a direct consequence of that, residential floor space being constructed, is cratering. You have to go back 15 years to find a comparable number.

We think all these metrics can go lower. If you use consumer confidence as a “lead,” then one can see how there may be much more pain ahead.

Goldman Sachs (GS), remarked that all these measures would raise GDP by 0.4%.

As our previous work has documented, China's real estate bubble is at least 10 times that of the US property bubble at the 2008 peak. China residential property consumed about 60%-70% of the world's iron ore if we use 2022 numbers. It just seems an awful bet here to think that everything will be hunky-dory just because China is now getting desperate and pumping some liquidity into the system. Realistically, residential prices need to fall 50% from here in Mainland China. The rental yields are so low that even extreme stimulus is not moving the needle.

Local bureaucrats are reconciling the demands from Beijing while trying to be prudent about costs. Buying apartments at this point makes little financial sense for those officials, as apartment prices are expected to drop at least another 30% in major cities before stabilizing, according to Jefferies Financial Group Inc.

Estimated returns from turning inventory into affordable housing are also below the cost of funding. Rental yields in China’s tier-1 cities averaged just 1.4% in 2023, compared with the central bank’s funding rate of 1.75%, according to Macquarie Group Ltd.

Source: BNN Bloomberg.

Logically, we need zero construction of new homes for the next decade to absorb what is available currently.

Verdict

We are not seeing anything, yet, that makes us turn down our bearish view on the macro side. The company, though, remains one of the best ways to play this, it has little margin compression risks (being a royalty setup) and also has a very clean balance sheet. Some time back, we had suggested a covered call trade to play this. This is working out quite nicely as it improves your yield and reduces your risk.

We would propose investors use this kind of setup on the next decline. We continue to rate Labrador Iron as a “hold” and note that it is less risky than some other well-known Iron Ore plays like BHP Group Limited (BHP).

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Muni Bonds: Don't Fight The Fed, Buy Dips
data.description:
Originally published on October 8, 2024

Market Overview

Municipal bonds bucked the seasonal trend and posted a strong performance in September. Rallying interest rates provided leadership as the Federal Reserve started its long awaited easing cycle by lowering the federal funds rate by 50 basis points to 4.75%-5.00% and indicated additional rate cuts in the months ahead. The S&P Municipal Bond Index returned 1.01%, bringing the year-to-date total return to 2.89%. Longer-duration bonds and higher-rated credits performed best.

Supply once again surpassed expectations as deals were pulled forward ahead of the election. Issuance totalled $46 billion, 19% above the five-year average, bringing the year-to-date total to $369 billion, up 40%, year over year. Meanwhile, demand picked up as investors shifted cash further out the curve, resulting in mutual funds receiving $4.6 billion in inflows. Consequently, deals were well absorbed, and oversubscribed 5.1 times on average, above the year-to-date average of 4.3 times.

While we expect heightened volatility between now and Election Day, the market could be poised for a strong finish to the year. November and December have posted positive performance in each of the past five years, cumulatively accounting for 135% of full-year total return on average. In addition, performance has historically been strong following the start of rate cutting cycles. For now, we will continue to strategically deploy cash by leveraging the large primary calendar and capitalizing on any concessions in the new issue market. As election-related uncertainties diminish, we expect to adopt a more aggressive stance toward the end of the year.

Strategy insights

We remain neutral duration overall but will look to add on weakness. We advocate a barbell yield curve strategy, pairing front-end exposure with the 15-20-year part of the curve. We prefer single-A rated credits but think high yield offers a good risk-reward opportunity, given attractive carry, favorable structures, and the ability to generate alpha through security selection.

Overweight

  • Essential-service revenue bonds
  • Suburban governments and school districts
  • Flagship universities
  • Select issuers in the high yield space
  • National and large regional health systems

Underweight

  • Speculative projects with weak sponsorship, unproven technology, or unsound feasibility studies
  • Senior living and long-term care facilities
  • Small private colleges
  • Stand-alone and rural health providers

Credit headlines

Hurricane Helene, the third hurricane to strike Florida’s Big Bend region within just over a year, caused unprecedented flooding across 600 miles and resulted in more than 130 fatalities. Predicted to be one of the most costly in U.S. history, initial damage estimates range from $145 billion to $160 billion, trailing only Hurricane Harvey's $190 billion in 2017. Areas typically unaffected by severe weather, such as inland and mountainous regions in the Carolinas and Tennessee, have also reported damage. The most affected insurers are Florida-only carriers like Citizens, the state's insurer of last resort. However, Citizens is financially prepared for Helene's aftermath with $15 billion available for claims. The Biden administration has declared Major Disaster zones in Florida, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia, unlocking federal funds for affected counties. Emergency Declarations, which release fewer funds compared to Major Declarations, have been issued in Tennessee and Virginia. Historically, long-term credit deterioration for debt-issuing public entities in affected areas has been minimal owing to federal and state aid, private insurance, and charitable donations. However, longterm impacts could include population migration and insurers' hesitation to operate in these areas, potentially leading to decreased property values and tax revenues.

The International Longshoremen’s Association agreed to suspend a strike that had briefly closed ports on the East and Gulf Coasts after receiving an improved wage offer from port employers. The strike had the potential to cause substantial economic losses, estimated at $4 billion to $5 billion per day. The union reached a tentative agreement on wages and its 45,000 members will return to work, with the current contract extended until January 15, 2025. The wage increase will raise the top longshoremen’s wage to just over $63 per hour at the end of a new six-year contract. The issue of automation at the ports and improved retirement benefits are still to be negotiated. Credit concerns for these seaports remain limited.

Municipal and Treasury yield movements

Municipal performance

Investment involves risk. The two main risks related to fixed income investing are interest rate risk and credit risk. Typically, when interest rates rise, there is a corresponding decline in the market value of bonds. Credit risk refers to the possibility that the issuer of the bond will not be able to make principal and interest payments. There may be less information available on the financial condition of issuers of municipal securities than for public corporations. The market for municipal bonds may be less liquid than for taxable bonds. A portion of the income from tax-exempt bonds may be taxable. Some investors may be subject to Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT). Capital gains distributions, if any, are taxable. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. This material is not intended to be relied upon as a forecast, research or investment advice, and is not a recommendation, offer or solicitation to buy or sell any securities or to adopt any investment strategy. The opinions expressed are as of Oct 7, 2024, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. The information and opinions contained in this material are derived from proprietary and nonproprietary sources deemed by BlackRock to be reliable, are not necessarily all-inclusive and are not guaranteed as to accuracy. There is no guarantee that any forecasts made will come to pass. Any investments named within this material may not necessarily be held in any accounts managed by BlackRock. Reliance upon information in this material is at the sole discretion of the reader.

©2024 BlackRock, Inc or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved. BlackRock is a trademark of BlackRock, Inc or its affiliates. All other trademarks are those of their respective owners. Prepared by BlackRock Investments, LLC, member FINRA.

Not FDIC Insured • May Lose Value • No Bank Guarantee

374000-0924

This post originally appeared on BlackRock.

data.title: Standard Lithium: Investing In Shovels
data.description:
Before we begin, realize that buried deeper in this article are points about Koch and also oil majors that might want access to Standard Lithium's (NYSE:SLI) DLE technology. This joint Koch and Standard Lithium DLE tech is the result of years of testing and has resulted in a deep understanding of extracting lithium from Arkansas brines. If an oil major like Exxon (XOM) [who owns land next door to Standard Lithium] were to pair up with the company, it could save that oil major considerable time and expense. Before we get to that point in detail, though, we need to frame the current news with Standard Lithium.

Growing M&A Activity in the Lithium Industry

Do you hear that sound? That roar is the sizzle that is starting to come back to the lithium sector. In a surprise deal, Arcadium Lithium (ALTM) was bought out by Rio Tinto (RIO) in a $6.7 billion acquisition. Then General Motors (GM) announced they were injecting $625 million into Lithium Americas (LAC). These moves are obviously attracting the attention of investors.

Additionally, the US government has realized the importance of critical mineral independence from China, and this is moving many projects forward (by Department of Defense and Department of Energy grants and loans). Acquisitions and industry funding has perked up a few ears. However, we are still a long way off from a lithium recovery, but maybe this is the mainstream catalyst we were waiting for.

Now the question is, who might be next? One intriguing stock would be Standard Lithium, which received a $225 million grant from the Department of Energy (DoE). The key word to focus on here is the word grant, as opposed to a loan [such as Lithium Americas is set to receive from the DoE]. Grants do not have to be paid back as opposed to a loan. Another interesting point to ponder is Standard's third lithium property does not have a partner. Again, various large companies in the area could court them for access to its SLI-Koch DLE technology. It is something to ponder as you read this article but moving back to the $225 million grant.

Why A $225 Million DoE Grant Matters To Standard Lithium

Obviously, a $225 million grant is rather important, but how does it actually factor into Standards plans? First, we need to realize that this grant applies to one of the three lithium projects that Standard has. This joint venture project is between Standard and Equinor (EQNR) and is called the SWA project (located in Arkansas).

What Is The SWA Project?

SWA stands for South West Arkansas project. Standard's partner Equinor (market cap $70.3 billion) took a 45% position in the SWA project and the East Texas lithium project. Details of the Equinor deal are:

"The Transaction includes Equinor's contribution of up to US$160 million, representing its total gross project-level investment and reflecting its 45% ownership stake in the two entities. This investment includes a US$30 million cash payment to Standard Lithium at closing, a work program solely funded by Equinor of US$60 million, representing a US$33 million carry by Equinor for Standard Lithium’s portion, and US$27 million for Equinor’s portion, at the South West Arkansas Project (SWA) and East Texas (ETX) properties (ETX and together with SWA, the “Projects”), and up to US$70 million in payments to Standard Lithium subject to both parties taking positive Final Investment Decisions. Standard Lithium and Equinor will each own 55% and 45% of the Projects respectively, with Standard Lithium retaining operatorship." - Source: Standard Lithium

Standard Lithium Project Expansion To 45,000 Tonnes

Project size matters. Of course, viability also matters, and SLI has very viable properties located in infrastructure rich Arkansas. Hence, it makes sense that we see this project doubling its potential tonnage to 45,000 tonnes via:

"The project’s design is being updated from its original Preliminary Feasibility Study (PFS), and now targets a larger total output of 45,000 tonnes per annum of lithium carbonate, to be developed in two phases of 22,500 tonnes each. A Definitive Feasibility Study (DFS) and Front-End Engineering Design (FEED) are currently underway to support this expansion."

Looking at infrastructure, we note the 110-acre property in blue (as opposed to leased property in tan) and we can see the property has road access via Highway 29 and, importantly, rail access slightly to the west.

Connect the Koch Dots

Companies have stages of growth. Founders can have particular skill sets that enable them to create and grow a company. Some founders can evolve with it, but at times it might make sense to bring in experience in order to save time and foster deep-seeded connections. Some might call it a pedigree change. Lithium Americas brought in Jonathan Evans, who had a history at Livent [now called Arcadium Lithium]

The point is sometimes you need to bring in people who have deep experience growing large companies and, in this case, Standard brought in a Koch executive with 28 years of experience. This is the same Koch that invested $100 million in Standard a few years ago. See a pattern here? It makes you wonder if Koch will want some of this action with time.

Standard Lithium Financial Status

Looking over the October filings, we can see that Standard has a healthy war chest of capital at its disposal:

Cash and working capital of C$52.9 million and C$39.6 million, respectively, as of June 30, 2024. The Company has no term or revolving debt obligations as of June 30, 2024.

Additionally, they have a conditional grant of $225 via the Department of Energy and per partner Equinor they have additional potential cash injections to both the projects and Standard Lithium directly:

"A work program solely funded by Equinor of US$60 million, representing a US$33 million carry by Equinor for Standard Lithium’s portion, and US$27 million for Equinor’s portion, at the South West Arkansas Project (SWA) and East Texas (ETX) properties (ETX and together with SWA, the “Projects”), and up to US$70 million in payments to Standard Lithium subject to both parties taking positive Final Investment Decisions."

Lithium Demand

Taking a logical long-term approach, it pays to bet with demand forecasts to some extent. Granted these are just long-term best guesses but according to Rio Tinto (via Benchmark Intelligence) we could see a demand situation as follows:

Risk To Standard Lithium

The biggest risk is the obvious one; the economy goes into a deep recession and EV sales suffer. Looking at another angle, though, Standard's lithium plants will not be operational for a few years at best. It is possible they might catch brighter economic times (or worse times). We have covered the financial position above. With deep pocketed Equinor owning 45% of two of the three lithium sites, we are confident Standard Lithium will keep moving forward to reach the goal of commercial lithium production. A secondary item to watch would be the ongoing royalty issues being ironed out by the Arkansas Oil & Gas Commission (AOGC) between the landowners on one side and the lithium miners on the other. I do not have a full understanding at the moment (to have a solid opinion) but it is something to be aware of. If the AOGC is unable to come up with a royalty rate that makes sense for both parties, investments might simply flow into the superior lithium grades in eastern Texas (where Standard and Equinor have a project too).

Will Oil Majors Court Standard Lithium For Its DLE Tech?

Right now, I am adding to my Standard Lithium position. Lithium is currently on a run with lithium investors in Arcadium Lithium selling and injecting profits into Standard Lithium [among others like Lithium Americas, Century Lithium (OTCQX:CYDVF) and even helping push up speculative Surge Battery Metals (OTCQX:NILIF)]. Realizing that two of the three lithium mines have a partner, the remaining mine site needs a partner with deep pockets. While the third site has the lowest grade lithium, one might view it as a carrot that Standard can dangle. Who might be interested and why? Well, Koch is an obvious choice, but also Exxon (XOM) could be a contender. Exxon just moved into the area by purchasing 120,000 acres. Albemarle (ALB) and Tetra Technologies (TTI) are also in the area. Do these companies have deep experience with DLE in Arkansas? I would guess not. Could they use a partner with years of DLE experience in this geographic area and with extracting lithium from Arkansas brines? I think so. Thus, the third Standard Lithium site could be the carrot that fosters that relationship.

Investing In Shovels

A reader asked me, "Why not just invest in GM?", in relation to the Lithium Americas and GM news. My answer is: I want to be invested in the shovels that power the car companies. Standard Lithium is one such potential lithium shovel provider.

Given the cash position, the $225 million DoE grant, and the favorable geographic area – not to mention lithium mining friendly locals – I see no reason not to buy and hold Standard Lithium at $2 and change. Of course, we should not forget partner Equinor and its 45% ownership of two lithium projects either, as that partnership de-risks the company. Out of all my lithium investments, Standard is one of my favorites. I will continue to expand upon my position in this future lithium giant.

If you would like to know more, read my previous coverage of SLI Properties and how Texas stacks up.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Tower's Multiyear Underperformance Will Continue
data.description:
American Tower (NYSE:AMT) is a $100 billion real estate investment trust that owns wireless and broadcast communication infrastructure. We recommended against investing in the company when we last discussed it almost 3 years ago, and since then, it has underperformed the S&P 500 by almost 40%. As we'll see throughout this article, the company remains an overvalued investment.

American Tower Portfolio

The company has a distributed portfolio spread across 25 countries with more than 200k assets.

The company has primarily international towers, with more than 220k spread across the U.S. & Canada along with the company's International operations. The company's U.S. & Canada operations are mainly suburban / rural locations. The company also owns 28 data centers, an exciting business at this time, but unfortunately facing competition from big tech companies owning their own assets.

Overall, we expect the company to see moderate growth opportunities in its existing businesses.

American Tower Financial Performance

The company has seen moderate revenue growth of ~4.6%, in line with inflation in the company's operations over the past year.

The company has seen continued strength for data centers as demand remains incredibly high and available sites remain limited. The company's total revenue growth has been supported by International growth being stronger than the company's US & Canada growth. We expect growth to moderate as inflation levels and the company's billings decline.

The company saw 8% growth in adjusted EBITDA. The company did manage to improve its cash margins some from almost 62% to almost 65%. That margin growth is impressive, however, with the company's already high margins, there's not a ton of room for growth to be seen here. That could moderate the company's future growth.

The company does have substantial debt to EBITDA at ~5x which is almost $40 billion in long-term debt. That's primarily fixed rate debt for the company but it's a massive amount of debt and a big impact on cash flow. The company's AFFO is $2.79 / share for the quarter, putting the company at a ~4.5% AFFO yield.

That's a relatively low yield and it shows the company's lofty valuation which we expect will continue moderating future returns.

American Tower Outlook

The company's outlook here has increased slightly primarily due to changes of its reserves for India.

However, that "India Revision" with the company's asset sale there to Brookfield is a one-time thing. The company's real expenses combined with FX have put a net negative pressure on the company's guidance which is something worth paying attention to in future years when that "revision" factor won't exist.

American Tower Financials

Financially, the company's lofty valuation has a substantial impact on its ability to drive future shareholder returns.

The company's hefty debt yield is evidenced through the fact that it keeps its weighted average debt term relatively low at <6 years. Many other investment grade companies have much longer term debt for stability. With expectations for interest rates to go down, that could help the company as it rolls debt over, but it still remains a risk for the company.

That's especially true that a lot of this debt likely came from a lower interest rate environment. The company's 2.8% dividend makes up almost 70% of its capital deployment, again an indication of the company's lofty valuation. The company's debt combined with this relatively high payout ratio should affect its ability to grow its dividend over the long term.

The company's $1.5 billion discretionary capital is affordable and could help support some future growth, but the company is facing rising competition in some of its fastest growing industries such as datacenters. Again that could present a downside threat.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that the company has a strong portfolio of long-lived assets and continues to generate reliable cash flow. In a market downturn, the company's reliable cash flow could enable it to outperform, making the company an investment that outperforms the market in your portfolio.

Conclusion

American Tower has underperformed versus the S&P 500 over the last 3 years, which was the last time we discussed the company. That's caused by the company's lofty valuation, with its substantial stability. That stability is exciting, however, the company is still riskier by itself versus the S&P 500, so it needs to generate higher returns.

With a relatively high payout yield for a dividend less than 3%, and a lofty debt load, we expect the company to continue underperforming the market. Threats to its growing datacenter business, as large companies own and construct their own, could also have a substantial impact. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Park Aerospace Growth Potential Weighed Down By Valuation Issues
data.description:
Thesis

Park Aerospace Corp. (NYSE:PKE), a well-established player in aerospace materials, has just released its second-quarter results for fiscal 2025. And the numbers are respectable enough:

GAAP earnings per share (EPS) landed at $0.10, and revenue reached $16.71 million, a sprightly 33.9% jump from last year.

Net earnings, disregarding any special items, came in at $2.1 million, while adjusted EBITDA clocked in at a tidy $3.21 million.

Looking at the bigger picture, while Park Aerospace's steady financials and involvement in key aerospace projects suggest room for growth, I think there are still some red flags: inconsistent dividend payouts, underutilized facilities, and questions around valuation make it clear that a cautious approach might be wise moving forward.

Park Aerospace's Long History

Park Aerospace Corp., founded in 1954, plays a big role in the aerospace industry. The company develops and manufactures advanced composite materials, mainly used in building composite structures for military and commercial aerospace applications. These materials, like hot-melt and solution prepreg composites, are key for making lightweight but tough components, including both primary and secondary aircraft structures.

Park Aerospace runs its operations out of a state-of-the-art facility in the delightfully unassuming middle-of-America location of Newton, Kansas, where they not only design and fabricate all sorts of high-tech composite parts and assemblies, but also dabble in low-volume tooling. The company has made quite a name for itself by taking on the kinds of complicated, niche projects that most other businesses tend to avoid. In doing so, they've carved out a reputation for being the go-to experts for all things tricky and demanding in aerospace manufacturing.

Dividend Performance and Volatility

While Park Aerospace has built a solid reputation as a leader in the aerospace sector, and given its long history, it's worth taking a step back to see the bigger picture. Many long-term investors are tied to this company, but PKE hasn't exactly kept pace with the S&P 500 (SPY) over time. From March 2005 to October 2024, its stock dropped from $20.53 to $14.64, resulting in an annual return of -1.71% (not counting dividends). In contrast, the S&P 500 delivered 8.85% per year during that same stretch. The point here is that underperformance highlights that Park hasn't added much value through price growth.

Dividends have been a big part of Park's returns: a $10K investment brought in $13.56K in dividends over time, nearly doubling what the S&P 500 offered. With an average dividend growth rate of 102.36% over 20 years, Park looks exceptionally strong in that area, especially when compared to the S&P's 6.90%.

But those dividends have been pretty inconsistent. There were some wild swings, like a 71.43% drop in 2021 (we can blame the pandemic for this), right after a massive 750% jump in 2018.

Plus, Park has often paid out more than it earned: in 2018, that jump translated to a payout ratio of 871.79%, showing the company was likely dishing out special dividends rather than steady, sustainable ones. On the surface, this suggests those large payouts mean the company isn't putting much back into growth opportunities.

Aerospace Sector Growth Opportunities

Despite Park Aerospace's rather erratic approach to dividends and shaky value growth, the second-quarter results for 2025 offer a few glimmers of hope-maybe even optimism. The company managed to pull in $16.71 million in revenue for the quarter, just sneaking past the upper end of its guidance. This little sales victory, coupled with an adjusted EBITDA of $3.2 million, which landed right on target, suggests the company might be finding its momentum footing.

A significant part of Park Aerospace's recent revenue bump came from $2.2 million in sales to ArianeGroup for something called RAYCARB C2B NG-a type of carbonized rayon, which is incredibly important for making heat-resistant products. These materials, known in the trade as ablative composites, are designed to protect things that get absurdly hot-like rocket motors and heat shields. Imagine it as a special coating that burns away bit by bit to protect what's underneath, much like a heat shield does when a spacecraft plummets back through the atmosphere. What makes this even more interesting is that Park Aerospace holds an exclusive agreement with ArianeGroup, making them the only company in North America that can distribute this specific material.

$6.5 Million Purchase Order

Additionally, Park's recent $6.5 million purchase order for materials linked to the GE9X engine program is a good reminder that they're very much in the mix when it comes to big aerospace projects. Even with Boeing's persistent struggles with the 777X aircraft, Park is stepping up to meet critical production needs.

The GE9X engine, designed specifically for the 777X, is the most powerful commercial engine ever built. Not only is it a behemoth, but it also comes with some serious fuel efficiency improvements. Park's contribution, supplying high-tech materials like carbon-fiber composites and ceramic matrix composites (CMCs), plays a critical role. These materials allow the engine to cope with extreme heat and pressure while making it more durable and fuel-efficient.

LEAP-1A Engine Growth Opportunity

Shifting our attention to the CFM LEAP-1A engine, which powers the Airbus A320neo family, we find what can only be described as a golden opportunity for both aircraft manufacturers and suppliers alike. At present, the LEAP-1A has managed to grab a commanding 64.4% share of the A320neo engine market, which is pretty impressive when you think about it. This engine is absolutely vital to CFM International-a joint venture between GE and Safran-because it delivers some rather remarkable benefits, including a 15-20% reduction in both fuel consumption and CO2 emissions compared to the clunkier, older engines of yesteryear. These advantages have firmly cemented the LEAP-1A as the go-to choice for airlines looking to outfit their narrow body fleets with something a little more eco-friendly and a lot more efficient.

Airbus Production Ramp-Up

Looking ahead, CFM is preparing for a rather sizable uptick in demand from Airbus, which has plans to ramp up production to 75 A320neo units per month by 2026-a significant leap from the current 48-50. If all goes according to plan, CFM could soon be cranking out over 1,000 LEAP-1A engines every year. This ramp-up promises to deliver a robust revenue stream for CFM as the LEAP-1A keeps powering an ever-growing fleet of A320neo aircraft.

Price Increase and Financial Health

Additionally, starting in January 2025, Park Aerospace will benefit from a 6.5% price increase under the MRAS/Park Long-Term Agreement, which should give profitability a nice little nudge in the right direction. Financially, the company is in pretty enviable shape, sitting on a tidy $72 million in cash with absolutely no long-term debt to speak of, giving them plenty of flexibility for future investments or shareholder returns. The company has also shown smart financial discipline through its share repurchase strategy, buying back 331,180 shares at an average price of $12.88, which, based on the current price of shares, demonstrates good capital management by avoiding overpaying.

As mentioned earlier, Park has been remarkably consistent when it comes to paying dividends, managing to keep that streak alive for an impressive 39 years. By the end of fiscal 2025, they're aiming to shell out $601.1 million in dividends.

On the sales front, things look equally promising: Park is expecting to rake in an extra $15 million from non-GE programs, and, for good measure, another $37.6 million from their GE-related ventures.

Valuation Challenges

To come up with a valuation for PKE, I looked at several data points. Instead of using Adjusted (Operating) Earnings Growth Rate, I calculated the CAGR from revenues, based on the Income Statement, from March 2015 to the TTM ending March 2024, and landed at around -9.62%. Even using operating income, it still rounds out to -9%.

With that in mind, the P/E ratio of 43.05 is pretty steep, which usually signals that investors are expecting big growth. But with an EPS of just 0.34, it's not exactly crushing it on the earnings side. That gap between the high P/E ratio and lower earnings suggests the stock might be overvalued.

Furthermore, that negative CAGR of -9.62% raises another red flag-losing value over time shows the company has struggled to grow or stay profitable. On the flip side, PKE's 3.42% dividend yield is that bright spot, and with a super-low long-term debt to capital ratio of 0.31%, it's clear they're keeping their balance sheet in good shape. That cautious approach could be helpful if the market hits turbulence or defense spending slows down. Overall, compared to the sector, PKE's normal P/E ratio of 24.83x suggests it's trading at a premium.

Profitability and Margin Pressure

While PKE is facing some challenges with its valuation and earnings, gross margin pressure has certainly made itself known: a 28.5% margin, falling short of its target 30%, mostly due to depreciation expenses from a new facility. To make matters worse, the sales mix for the quarter wasn't doing PKE any favors either. The ArianeGroup sales turned out to be low-margin, which didn't exactly help the bottom line.

The new production facility hasn't hit full utilization yet, which is putting more pressure on margins until production picks up. Until they can ramp up production, the facility is more of a cost burden than a profit booster, which isn't doing short-term profitability any favors. Moreover, Park missed out on $600,000 in missile shipments this quarter due to international shipping and supply chain disruptions, highlighting the ongoing operational challenges the aerospace sector is dealing with.

Automation and Future Projects

While automation could be a game-changer down the road, Park isn't exactly seeing huge efficiency gains from it right now, which could make things a bit tricky in terms of staying competitive. Other companies, who've jumped on the automation bandwagon a little quicker, are probably looking a lot more cutting-edge by comparison. To add insult to injury, the Park also got slapped with $46,000 in storm-related expenses, further disrupting operations.

Although Park's cash reserves are looking healthy presently, they've got $20 million in future expenses on the horizon. This includes $7.5 million for the Solution Treater project and another $5 million tied up in an OEM partnership. All of this could start to tighten up their liquidity. And then there are the supply chain headaches that won't go away-things like material and engine shortages that continue to threaten revenue and mess with operational forecasts.

Lastly, Park is still holding on to hope for their "Juggernaut" program, which they believe could be a real growth driver. But the timeline for this big initiative is still murky, and with all the other challenges swirling around in the industry, it's hard to say exactly when this juggernaut will get rolling.

Final Takeaway

I'd rate PKE a "Hold." The company certainly has its strengths-it's carved out a nice little niche in aerospace, keeps operations steady, and sits on a solid balance sheet with absolutely no debt, which is always a comfort. But, as with anything that looks too good to be true, there are a few cracks. Overvaluation is one, and the profitability squeeze is another, largely due to underused facilities and, of course, the ever-reliable chaos that supply chain disruptions bring.

Over the long run, Park Aerospace Corp. seems to be on firm footing, especially with its involvement in some high-profile aerospace projects. But in the short term, things are looking a bit murky. So, for now, the best move is to wait and watch.
